summary: Closed, unmerged PR adding missing implementations for lb_80019628, lbMemory_80015320, and lbMemory_8001564C, plus a small matching improvement in fn_80015184. The work focused on library-side memory/AR allocation behavior and a timing/PAD sampling update path, with one new lbmemory.h prototype. Evidence is limited to the PR body and diff excerpt; there were no review comments in the provided slice.
what_changed: Implemented lb_80019628 in src/melee/lb/lb_0192.c. The function updates lb_804329F0 timing state, derives a PAD sampling rate from OS_TIMER_CLOCK / ticks clamped to 11, calls PADSetSamplingRate when the rate changes, and manages an OS periodic alarm using OSCancelAlarm, OSCreateAlarm, and OSSetPeriodicAlarm with lb_80019900 as handler.

Implemented lbMemory_80015320 in src/melee/lb/lbmemory.c. The function walks Handle chains, updates g_alloc.x6E4, handles a nonzero cancelflag with an assert, chooses between HSD_DevComRequest for source addresses below 0x80000000 and a delayed OSAlarm-based transfer path for other addresses, then invokes the allocator callback stored in g_alloc.x6E8 when traversal completes.
Moved or hoisted lbmemory.c static data and struct declarations needed by the new implementation, including lbl_803BA2C0 and struct LBMgr.
Changed fn_80015184 alarm rescheduling from a raw constant and NULL handler to OSSetAlarm(&temp_r3->alarm, OS_MSEC_TO_TICKS(3), fn_80015184), described by the PR body as a small matching improvement.

Implemented lbMemory_8001564C, initializing AR arena bounds with ARAlloc, ARFree, and ARGetSize; setting up free Handle lists over g_alloc.x8 and g_alloc.x638; and initializing g_alloc.x69C through lbMemory_80014E24.
Added the lbMemory_80015320 prototype to src/melee/lb/lbmemory.h.
smart_moves: Replaced a magic alarm tick constant in fn_80015184 with OS_MSEC_TO_TICKS(3), improving readability and tying the expression to Dolphin OS timing semantics while also supplying fn_80015184 as the handler.
Preserved allocator struct sizing with STATIC_ASSERT(sizeof(g_alloc) == 0x6F0), which is important when using offset-based fields such as the LBMgr area at g_alloc + 0x6A0.

Modeled the async transfer manager as struct LBMgr with OSAlarm, src, dst, size, offset, callback argument, and callback fields, making the alarm/devcom behavior more understandable than raw pointer arithmetic alone.
Used alignment expression ((size + 0x1F) & ~0x1F) before HSD_DevComRequest and delayed transfer setup, a likely matching- and hardware-relevant detail for AR/devcom transfer sizes.
Added the public header declaration for lbMemory_80015320 only after giving it a concrete non-static signature, avoiding an implementation/header mismatch.
decomp_lessons: When a function depends on an embedded manager inside a larger global allocator, a small local struct such as LBMgr can make offset-based code readable while preserving exact layout assumptions.
For matching alarm code, prefer SDK macros such as OS_MSEC_TO_TICKS when the generated code matches; they communicate intent better than unexplained constants.
AR/devcom memory functions often require careful address-domain checks. This PR distinguishes addresses below 0x80000000 from normal cached memory and uses HSD_DevComRequest for the former.
Free-list initialization over fixed backing arrays can be represented by typed Handle casts and explicit loop bounds, but the bounds and final NULL termination are critical matching details.
Keep assertion strings and file-name statics close to original evidence. The diff preserved lbmemory.c string usage and assert line numbers in lbMemory_80015320.
naming_conventions: Unidentified library functions retain address-based names such as lb_80019628, lbMemory_80015320, lbMemory_8001564C, and fn_80015184.
The memory subsystem uses the camel-cased lbMemory_ prefix for functions in lbmemory.c.
Global allocator state is named g_alloc, while unknown struct fields retain offset-style names such as x6E4, x6E8, x698_free_heap, x69C, x0_arenaLo, and x4_arenaHi.
The timing/global state in lb_0192.c remains named lb_804329F0 with offset-style fields like x38, x40, x48, and x0.
assembly_or_matching_tactics: Introduced struct LBMgr to describe a block within g_alloc at offset 0x6A0, allowing field accesses for alarm transfer state while keeping allocator size fixed.
Used OSDisableInterrupts and OSRestoreInterrupts around LBMgr transfer-state initialization, matching an interrupt-safe setup sequence.
Rounded transfer sizes up to 0x20-byte boundaries with ((u32) arg1->x8_hi + 0x1F) & ~0x1F.
Used OS_MSEC_TO_TICKS(3) for a recurring delayed transfer alarm in both lbMemory_80015320 and fn_80015184.
In lb_80019628, derived PAD sampling rate as OS_TIMER_CLOCK / ticks and clamped it to 11 before calling PADSetSamplingRate, matching the apparent hardware rate-limiting behavior.
In lbMemory_8001564C, initialized two Handle freelists manually with fixed counts 0x81 and 7 and explicit NULL terminators.
review_feedback: No review comments were present in the provided PR slice.
The PR was closed with merged_at null, so the slice does not establish that these changes were accepted as-is.
follow_up_queries: Find later accepted or merged PRs touching lbMemory_80015320 or lbMemory_8001564C to see whether this closed PR's implementations were reused or corrected.
Search for subsequent renames or struct refinements for struct Allocator, struct LBMgr, and lb_804329F0 fields.
Check whether OSSetAlarm with OS_MSEC_TO_TICKS(3) and fn_80015184 became the final matching form after this PR.
Look for review or issue discussion outside the provided slice explaining why PR #2490 was closed unmerged.
